notes: 13-clue draft for music_performing_arts (11 active clues at time of authoring — most under-covered non-geography category). All clues written from facts in data/sourcing/docs/music_performing_arts/. Subcategory spread: Classical Composers (5), Opera (1), Jazz & Blues (1), Popular Music (2), Broadway & Musicals (2), Theater (2). Value range $200–$1000; genuine $1000 uses the Mahler Catholic-conversion angle (obscure biographical entry point). Avoided all answers already in the June-16 draft: Beethoven's Fifth, 1812 Overture, Bolero, Mahler (active-bank answers), Sweeney Todd, Oh Mary!, Hedwig, Annie Oakley, Annie Get Your Gun, Big Lebowski.

Note: Gustav Mahler ($1000) clues from a different biographical angle than the $800 clue in the active bank. The 'Catholic conversion' entry point is genuinely obscure and earns $1000 per edit-lessons §1. Skiffle ($800) is the Beatles' origin style — most players won't know this.

questions.clue: Each summer, a Bavarian town on the Red Main river hosts an annual festival devoted exclusively to the operas of this 19th-century German Romantic composer.

citations.title: Bayreuth
citations.snippet: Bayreuth is a town in northern Bavaria, Germany, on the Red Main river in a valley between the Franconian Jura and the Fichtel Mountains. The town's roots date back to 1194. In the 21st century, it is the capital of Upper Franconia and has

questions.reviewer_note: Source: bayreuth.json. Fact: 'It hosts the annual Bayreuth Festival, at which performances of operas by the 19th-century German composer Richard Wagner are presented.'

questions.clue: This Russian composer wrote a concert overture in 1880 commemorating his country's successful defense against a French invasion; despite its patriotic theme, the piece later became a staple of American Independence Day celebrations.

citations.title: 1812 Overture
citations.snippet: The Year 1812, Solemn Overture, Op. 49, popularly known as the 1812 Overture, is a concert overture in E♭ major written in 1880 by Russian composer Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky. The piece commemorates Russia's successful defence against the Fre

questions.reviewer_note: Source: the-1812-overture.json. Answer is the COMPOSER (Tchaikovsky), not the work title. Clue describes the 1812 Overture without naming it.

questions.clue: Along with The Nutcracker and The Sleeping Beauty, this completes the trio of Tchaikovsky's most celebrated ballet scores.

citations.title: 1812 Overture
citations.snippet: The Year 1812, Solemn Overture, Op. 49, popularly known as the 1812 Overture, is a concert overture in E♭ major written in 1880 by Russian composer Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky. The piece commemorates Russia's successful defence against the Fre

questions.reviewer_note: Source: the-1812-overture.json. The three Tchaikovsky ballets are explicitly named in the extract. Clue gives two and asks for the third.

questions.clue: Illness ended this French composer's ability to write music after he completed a 1928 work for large orchestra — one of his most famous compositions.

citations.title: Boléro
citations.snippet: Boléro is a 1928 work for large orchestra by French composer Maurice Ravel. It is one of Ravel's most famous compositions. It was also one of his last completed works before illness diminished his ability to write music.

questions.reviewer_note: Source: bolero.json. Answer is the COMPOSER (Ravel), not Boléro. Clue describes Boléro without naming it.

questions.clue: Along with Alban Berg and Anton Webern, this composer formed the Second Viennese School — a modernist movement whose members were among the immediate successors of Gustav Mahler.

citations.title: Gustav Mahler
citations.snippet: Gustav Mahler was an Austro-Bohemian Romantic composer, and one of the leading conductors of his generation. As a composer he acted as a bridge between the 19th-century tradition and the modernism of the early 20th century. While in his lif

questions.reviewer_note: Source: mahler.json. Clue names Berg and Webern, asks for Schoenberg (the third, arguably leading figure). $800 because most casual fans know the Second Viennese School but not all three members by name.

questions.clue: Born Jewish in Bohemia, this Austro-Bohemian conductor converted to Catholicism in 1897 to secure his appointment as director of Vienna's imperial opera house, then endured a decade of anti-Semitic opposition during his tenure there.

citations.title: Gustav Mahler
citations.snippet: Gustav Mahler was an Austro-Bohemian Romantic composer, and one of the leading conductors of his generation. As a composer he acted as a bridge between the 19th-century tradition and the modernism of the early 20th century. While in his lif

questions.reviewer_note: Source: mahler.json. $1000 — genuinely obscure biographical hook (Jewish-born conductor converts to Catholicism, still faces anti-Semitism). Different angle from the active-bank $800 clue about Mahler as a bridge between Romanticism and modernism.

questions.clue: This American jazz saxophonist, bandleader, and composer is regarded as among the most influential and acclaimed figures in the history of jazz and 20th-century music.

citations.title: John Coltrane
citations.snippet: John William Coltrane was an American jazz saxophonist, bandleader, and composer. He is among the most influential and acclaimed figures in the history of jazz and 20th-century music.

questions.reviewer_note: Source: john-coltrane.json. Thin doc — clue is a close paraphrase of the extract. $200 is correct; jazz fans will get this immediately.

questions.clue: This English rock band, formed in Liverpool in 1960, comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr, and is regarded as the most influential band in popular music.

citations.title: The Beatles
citations.snippet: The Beatles were an English rock band formed in Liverpool in 1960. The band comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison and Ringo Starr. They are regarded as the most influential band in popular music and were integral to the dev

questions.reviewer_note: Source: the-beatles.json. Straightforward $200 recall clue — naming all four members is the anchor.

questions.clue: Before expanding into rock 'n' roll, classical experimentation, and psychedelia, the Beatles had their musical roots in this working-class British style, also performed alongside beat music.

citations.title: The Beatles
citations.snippet: The Beatles were an English rock band formed in Liverpool in 1960. The band comprised John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison and Ringo Starr. They are regarded as the most influential band in popular music and were integral to the dev

questions.reviewer_note: Source: the-beatles.json. 'Skiffle' not in clue. $800 — most players know the Beatles were from Liverpool but don't know they started as a skiffle band. Good non-obvious hook.

questions.clue: This filmmaker both wrote and directed the 2001 film about an East German rock singer while reprising the same lead role he had originated in the 1998 stage musical of the same name.

citations.title: Hedwig and the Angry Inch (film)
citations.snippet: Hedwig and the Angry Inch is a 2001 American musical comedy-drama film written for the screen and directed by John Cameron Mitchell. Based on Mitchell's and Stephen Trask's 1998 stage musical Hedwig and the Angry Inch, it accompanies Hedwig

questions.reviewer_note: Source: hedwig-and-the-angry-inch.json. Answer is the PERSON (Mitchell), not the film/musical title. $600 — requires knowing the triple-threat role (writer/director/star) and that he originated the stage role.

questions.clue: In the 2001 film, John Cameron Mitchell's character Hedwig Robinson fronts this backing band as she shadows her former protégé's tour.

citations.title: Hedwig and the Angry Inch (film)
citations.snippet: Hedwig and the Angry Inch is a 2001 American musical comedy-drama film written for the screen and directed by John Cameron Mitchell. Based on Mitchell's and Stephen Trask's 1998 stage musical Hedwig and the Angry Inch, it accompanies Hedwig

questions.reviewer_note: Source: hedwig-and-the-angry-inch.json. Answer is the band name. Distinct from clue #010 (which asks for Mitchell, the person). Both from same doc — different facts cited.

questions.clue: This American comedian wrote and starred in a 2024 Broadway comedy, becoming the first non-binary performer to win the Tony Award for Best Actor in a Play.

citations.title: Oh, Mary!
citations.snippet: Oh, Mary! is a comedy play written and originally performed by American comedian Cole Escola. The show opened on Broadway on July 11, 2024, at the Lyceum Theatre, transferring from its off-Broadway run at the Lucille Lortel Theatre, earning

questions.reviewer_note: Source: oh-mary.json. Answer is the PERSON (Cole Escola), not the show title Oh, Mary! $600 — recent (2024) and specific enough for that value.

questions.clue: Named after a legendary English actor and director, this British theater prize was awarded for Best Entertainment or Comedy Play in 2026 to Cole Escola's comedy after its West End transfer.

citations.title: Oh, Mary!
citations.snippet: Oh, Mary! is a comedy play written and originally performed by American comedian Cole Escola. The show opened on Broadway on July 11, 2024, at the Lyceum Theatre, transferring from its off-Broadway run at the Lucille Lortel Theatre, earning

questions.reviewer_note: Source: oh-mary.json. $800 — requires knowing the British theater prize is named after Laurence Olivier. Distinct answer from #012 (Escola the person).
